verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Material Storage) clearly shows a flat concrete/stone-tile roof surface with no visible slope. The rooftop is level with no pitch. Adjacent water tank on neighboring building confirms flat RCC rooftop typical of the area.
verdicts.rationale: Flat grey concrete/tile surface visible on rooftop photo on page 8; consistent with RCC Flat Roof answer.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Material Storage) shows the flat roof surface; water tank visible on a neighboring building (not on this roof). Blue tarpaulin and temporary construction materials stored on roof edge but no permanent obstructions in the proposed PV area. Page 13 (Proposed PV Area) overlay confirms the panel zone is clear.
verdicts.rationale: Roof appears clear of permanent obstructions in the proposed PV area. Only temporary construction materials present, which are not permanent fixtures.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-12 show SketchUp 3D side-view renderings of the MS elevated structure on the flat RCC roof. The panels in the side-view rendering appear to have a low tilt angle visually consistent with approximately 10 degrees. Vertical post heights and diagonal bracing dimensions are detailed in the structure drawings.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp side views (pages 9-12) show a low-angle tilt on the MS elevated structure consistent with a 10-degree setting. No actual site photo of installed panels available to confirm precise angle.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Site Layout Top View) shows all 8 panels arranged on a single continuous flat roof area in the SketchUp top view. Page 8 (Material Storage) photo shows a single flat rooftop space. No second distinct roof section is visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: All panels shown on a single flat RCC rooftop in both the actual roof photo and the SketchUp top-view layout diagram.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-12 show detailed SketchUp renderings of an MS fabricated elevated frame on the flat RCC roof: vertical column legs (100x100mm per structure details 5), horizontal rails (50x80mm per structure details 4), diagonal bracing, and panel clamp connections. Rail spacing dimensions shown (930-935mm between rails, 1404mm purlin spacing). The structure clearly uses long MS rails with L-clamp style connections.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp structure drawings (pages 9-12) clearly show MS fabricated elevated structure with long rail system and clamp connections, fully consistent with 'Long rails with L clamps / MS fabrication structure'.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Site Layout Top View) includes a compass rose with 'N' label clearly pointing toward the bottom of the diagram. The panel array in the top view is positioned with the structure sloping toward the south (away from North). Side view renderings on page 9 show panels tilted in a consistent direction. The top-view compass and panel arrangement are consistent with a southward-facing orientation.
verdicts.rationale: Compass rose in the SketchUp top-view layout (page 8) confirms the reference direction; panel tilt arrangement is consistent with south-facing orientation.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Staircase Details) shows a photo of a narrow staircase with metal diamond-plate steps and iron grille railing leading to the roof, with a width measurement of 0.3m annotated. Page 6 (top photo) shows an interior marble staircase for small item access.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase clearly documented in page 7 photo with 0.3m width annotation; consistent with designer's answer of 'Staircase'.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 (Building Exterior) clearly shows active construction: wooden cladding/paneling being installed on the facade, construction debris on the ground, a worker visible, and blue safety netting on the right side. Page 13 (Proposed PV Area) shows wooden scaffolding and construction materials at the building. Page 16 (Earth Pit Location) shows freshly excavated red soil. Critically, the designer's own notes on Electrical Q3 and Q4 explicitly state 'Building is under construction.' Page 17 (Observations) states 'At the time of installation wooden partition has to be removed by the customer.'
verdicts.rationale: Photo evidence on pages 5, 13, and 16 clearly shows active construction. The designer's own electrical section notes (Q3 and Q4) explicitly state 'Building is under construction,' directly contradicting the 'No' answer given here.

verdicts.question: In an under-construction building, are existing conduit pipes available for AC, DC cables, and earthing?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 13-15 (Cable Routing) show an existing external conduit pipe on the building wall planned for cable routing. However, this appears to be the planned external route rather than pre-installed internal building conduit. No interior pre-installed conduit pipes are visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: Building is under construction per photo evidence (contradicting Q11 answer). Whether interior conduit pipes are pre-installed cannot be clearly determined from the exterior routing photos. External conduit pipe visible is for planned routing, not pre-installed building infrastructure.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.designer_answer: Height of the building is 12m, it has G+1+headroom
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 (Building Exterior) shows: ground floor (entrance level with wooden cladding, a person visible at ground level), first floor above (with windows and balcony/metal railing), and a partial upper headroom structure visible at the top. Page 6 (bottom photo - exterior) confirms the three-level building profile from the street view.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photos show Ground floor + one full floor + headroom structure, consistent with G+1+headroom. 12m height for this floor count is plausible for Indian residential construction.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5-6 show building exterior photos showing a residential building with RCC flat roof, G+1+headroom profile, and staircase access, broadly consistent with the project description. Note that the construction status discrepancy flagged in Q11 is a concern.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photos are broadly consistent with the described residential building profile (floor count, roof type, access). The photos match the general building description.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 6 (bottom photo - Material Delivery Route) shows overhead cables/wires visible near the top of the frame adjacent to the building near the palm tree. These appear to be standard residential service distribution wires rather than high-tension lines. No thick bundled HT cables, insulator strings, or HT tower/pole structures are visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: Overhead wires visible in page 6 photo appear to be low-voltage service lines. No HT infrastructure (heavy bundled cables, insulator strings, HT poles/towers) visible in any photo.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 (Material Delivery Route) shows the ground-level unloading area at the building entrance with an accessible doorway. Page 6 shows the planned routes for both small items (internal staircase) and large items (exterior building face route), indicating planned delivery from street level.
verdicts.rationale: Material delivery route photos confirm accessible ground-level entry and designated unloading area. Standard residential street access to the site is implied.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Staircase Details) shows the rooftop access staircase is only 0.3m wide — very narrow for material handling. Pages 13-14 (top photo) show a very narrow side passage between buildings planned for large item (panel) lifting route. Page 5 shows active construction activity at the building which may further impede movement.
verdicts.rationale: The 0.3m narrow staircase (page 7) and tight side passage between buildings (pages 13-14) visible in photos could present significant lifting obstacles for 2382mm long solar panels. Warrants manual verification of whether the large-item route can safely accommodate panel dimensions.

verdicts.question: Where will the Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery, etc. be located?
verdicts.designer_answer: Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery is located near the ground floor.
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 (bottom photo) shows overlay labels 'INVERTER' (purple box) and 'BATTERY' (green box) positioned at what appears to be the ground floor interior area near a wooden partition wall, with a 'UPS Point' also labeled. Page 15 shows additional equipment location photos at the building exterior/ground level.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 photo explicitly shows inverter and battery placement overlays at ground floor level, directly consistent with the designer's answer of ground floor location.

verdicts.question: Is a canopy required for protecting the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 (bottom photo) shows the inverter and battery location is under an existing wooden ceiling/overhang structure. The equipment area appears to be sheltered from direct weather exposure by the existing covered/canopied structure of the building.
verdicts.rationale: Inverter/battery location on page 14 appears to be under an existing sheltered area (wooden ceiling visible above), making an additional canopy unnecessary.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Earth pits are located near the ground floor (toward south direction)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 (Earth Pit Location) clearly shows an annotated photo with labeled markers: 'DC Earth Pit', 'AC Earth Pit', and 'LA Pit' at the base of the south-facing wall of the building. Red excavated soil and brick work visible at the pit locations.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 photo shows clearly labeled earth pit locations at ground level on the south side of the building, directly consistent with the designer's description of 'ground floor, toward south direction'.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo specifically showing internet router location or any related network infrastructure in the TSVR document.
verdicts.rationale: No photo available to verify router location. This is an administrative/scope answer that cannot be visually confirmed from TSVR photos.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No WhatsApp screenshot or location pin photo visible in the TSVR document. GPS coordinates (13.02387641246595, 77.64151377032137) and a Google Maps link are provided in the project information section, indicating the location was recorded.
verdicts.rationale: This is a process confirmation that cannot be verified from TSVR photos. GPS coordinates in the project header suggest site location was captured, but no WhatsApp pin screenshot is provided.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: LT w/o CT (5 - 34.999)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Meter Box) shows the open meter cubicle with a digital meter unit (green LED display segment visible at the top of the meter) and multiple circuit breakers/phase connections below, consistent with a three-phase arrangement. The meter form factor matches a standard three-phase digital utility meter.
verdicts.rationale: Meter box photo on page 7 shows a digital display meter with multi-phase wiring arrangement, consistent with a three-phase digital meter. Exact CT rating class cannot be verified from photo resolution alone.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Meter Box) and page 16 (Main Meter overlay photo) both show the same meter cubicle. The cubicle contains a digital-display meter with visible multi-phase wiring. Page 16 clearly labels 'MAIN METER' and 'SOLAR METER' positions within the same cubicle structure.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photos on pages 7 and 16 show a digital three-phase type meter consistent with the stated meter type; cubicle configuration matches the described installation.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 shows the meter panel with 'MAIN METER' labeled (red overlay at top) and 'SOLAR METER' space identified (blue overlay below) within the same cubicle. The designated termination area for the solar meter is clearly marked in the photo.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 photo with labeled overlays confirms the termination point (solar meter space) has been identified and marked in the main meter panel.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Meter Box) shows the meter cubicle open with accessible internals. Page 16 shows the open meter box with clearly labeled main meter and solar meter spaces. The cubicle door is open and interior is accessible and visible.
verdicts.rationale: Open meter cubicle photos on pages 7 and 16 confirm the termination point is visible and accessible, not sealed.

verdicts.question: Is a Lightning Arrestor (LA) already available at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No lightning arrestor visible in any equipment photos (pages 14-16). No LA spike or ground rod visible in roof photos (pages 8, 13). Page 16 (Earth Pit Location) shows a planned 'LA Pit' marker, confirming LA is planned for installation but not currently present.
verdicts.rationale: No LA visible in any site photos. The planned 'LA Pit' marker in page 16 confirms the LA needs to be installed as part of the project, consistent with 'No' existing LA.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 clearly shows a photo of the meter panel with 'MAIN METER' labeled at top and a 'SOLAR METER' overlay below it in the same cubicle, indicating a designated empty space for the solar meter. The cubicle appears to have sufficient vertical space below the existing meter.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 photo with labeled overlays explicitly shows the solar meter space available below the main meter in the same cubicle, directly confirming the 'Yes' answer.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is an LA extension provided at the far end?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-12 show SketchUp renderings of the elevated MS structure but no LA spike, rod, or extension is visible in the renderings. Page 8 (actual roof photo) does not show any LA spike at the far end of the proposed structure area. No dedicated photo of LA extension is included in the TSVR.
verdicts.rationale: No photo or SketchUp rendering clearly shows an LA extension at the far end of the elevated MS structure. Cannot confirm from available visual documentation despite designer's 'Yes' answer.

verdicts.question: Are there any challenges with respect to Installation and Maintenance activities?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 shows a very narrow staircase (0.3m width) for roof access. Pages 13-14 show a tight side passage between buildings for the large-item delivery route. Page 5 confirms active construction with wooden partitions. Page 17 (Observations) explicitly notes 'At the time of installation wooden partition has to be removed by the customer.'
verdicts.rationale: The 0.3m narrow staircase, tight side passage, active construction, and the wooden partition requiring customer removal (per page 17 observations) suggest potential installation challenges that contradict the 'No' answer. Warrants manual review.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 (Staircase Details) shows the roof access staircase is annotated as only 0.3m wide with metal diamond-plate steps and iron grille railing. A 0.3m staircase width is extremely narrow for workers carrying solar panels (2382mm long) or heavy equipment.
verdicts.rationale: The 0.3m staircase width shown in the page 7 photo is very narrow and could present a safety risk for workers carrying heavy equipment to the roof. This warrants a manual safety review despite the designer's 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Material Storage) shows the flat rooftop with open space and a blue tarpaulin covering materials already stored horizontally on the roof surface. The flat RCC roof has sufficient open area for panels to be laid flat.
verdicts.rationale: Page 8 photo confirms the flat RCC roof has open space suitable for horizontal panel storage, consistent with 'Horizontally sleeping' answer.

verdicts.question: Is ventilation available in the battery location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 (bottom photo) shows the battery location (green 'BATTERY' overlay) adjacent to a louvered wooden partition/wall with visible slatted structure that could provide natural ventilation. However, the photo angle does not clearly show the full ventilation arrangement around the battery zone.
verdicts.rationale: Battery location photo (page 14) shows an adjacent louvered wooden structure that may provide some ventilation, but the adequacy of ventilation cannot be clearly confirmed from the available photo angle and resolution.

verdicts.question: Should the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB be installed on a metal grill or wall mount?
verdicts.designer_answer: Inverters/ACDB/D CDB be installed on a wall mounted
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 (bottom photo) shows the 'INVERTER' overlay positioned against a wall/wooden partition surface, indicating wall mounting. Page 15 (equipment location photos) show the planned equipment mounting on the building wall exterior.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 shows the inverter overlay against a wall surface, and page 15 shows equipment planned on building wall, both consistent with wall mounting.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 shows the solar meter space within the main meter cubicle with a 'SOLAR METER' overlay, but no dimension measurement or scale reference is visible in the photo to verify the 400x400mm dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Photo shows the solar meter placement area but provides no scale or measurement reference to verify the 400x400mm dimensions stated by the designer.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 (Earth Pit Location) shows the excavated earth pit area with labeled 'DC Earth Pit', 'AC Earth Pit', and 'LA Pit' markers on freshly excavated red soil. No measurement scale or dimension reference is visible in the photo to verify the 500x499mm dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Earth pit location photo on page 16 confirms pit locations exist but provides no scale reference to verify the stated 500x499mm dimensions.

verdicts.question: Is LAN cable under customer scope?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 17 (Observations) lists 'Wi-Fi upto solar meter is under customer scope' — this addresses wireless connectivity, not LAN cable. No specific photo showing LAN cable routing or LAN-related infrastructure visible in the TSVR.
verdicts.rationale: No photo evidence to verify LAN cable scope arrangement. Administrative answer requires manual confirmation. Note that page 17 mentions Wi-Fi as customer scope but the designer states LAN cable is EcoSoch scope ('No' to customer scope).

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (Material Storage) shows neighboring building walls and a water tank on an adjacent roof visible near the proposed installation area. Page 5 shows trees adjacent to the building facade. No dedicated shadow analysis photo or sun path study is included in the TSVR. The site is in a dense residential area (HRBR Layout, Bengaluru) where neighboring structures could potentially cast shadows.
verdicts.rationale: No dedicated shadow analysis photo provided in the TSVR. Neighboring buildings (page 8) and trees (page 5) are present near the site and could cast shadows on the panel area. Cannot confirm 'No shadow' without a shadow analysis study or dedicated shadow photo.

verdicts.question: Is there any trench work to be done to carry the AC cable to the meter location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 13-15 (Cable Routing) show the AC cable routed along the exterior building wall via a surface-mounted conduit pipe, running from the rooftop downward to the meter location. No underground trench is shown. All cable routing is on the building facade surface.
verdicts.rationale: Cable routing photos (pages 13-15) clearly show the AC cable planned to run along the exterior wall surface via conduit pipe — no underground trenching required, consistent with the 'No' answer.
